completeness.rationale: All 7 expected extracts are present: 2-4 hour delays, ~1 week onset, morning-worse/afternoon-better pattern, digest email as the cause, queue congestion, transactional vs bulk pipeline competition, and prior 1-2 minute baseline. The agent also captured additional useful information: afternoon delay specifics (~20-30 min), system-wide confirmation across multiple users, and content-integrity check (notifications look normal when they arrive).

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies the digest email feature as the root cause and queue congestion as the mechanism — matching acceptable path #2. The recommended fix even mentions giving digest emails a lower-priority queue, which is directionally aligned with the actual fix. The slight inaccuracy is introducing email-provider rate limiting as a co-hypothesis alongside queue congestion; the actual mechanism is priority inversion within a single internal queue, not external SMTP throttling. This is a minor misdirection but doesn't undermine the core diagnosis.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent systematically ruled out user-side batching behavior, confirmed the timing correlation between digest delivery and notification delays, verified content integrity to distinguish sending vs. delivery failure, and confirmed the issue is system-wide. One missed avenue: asking when the digest feature was launched could have independently confirmed the 1-week timeline without relying on the reporter's uncertain memory. Asking about digest recipient volume would also have quantified queue pressure. Still, the agent had a solid basis for resolution.

economy.rationale: Three rounds of questioning, each well-targeted and often combining two related sub-questions into one efficient ask. No redundancy; each turn extracted distinctly different information (environmental changes + behavior pattern → timing correlation + content integrity → scope/system-wide). Nothing wasted.

question_quality.rationale: Questions demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning. Q1 cleverly combined 'what changed' with 'do you batch assignments at 9am' — ruling out both external triggers and user-side explanations in one shot. Q2 was particularly insightful: asking the reporter to correlate the digest arrival time with when delayed notifications trickle in is something the reporter would never have volunteered and directly confirmed the causal link. Q3 efficiently distinguished account-specific vs. systemic failure.

actionability.rationale: The summary is highly actionable: it includes a clear root cause hypothesis, step-by-step reproduction instructions, a multi-step recommended fix with concrete options (separate queues, lower priority, off-peak scheduling, rate limiting), a proposed test case with measurable success criteria, and explicitly listed information gaps for the developer to investigate. A developer could begin the investigation and likely the fix immediately.

notable_strengths: The agent surfaced the digest email connection in the very first exchange — the reporter mentioned it unprompted but only after being asked about recent environmental changes, which the agent proactively framed
Q2 asking the reporter to correlate digest arrival time with the delayed notification pattern was genuinely diagnostic and produced the clearest evidence linking the two
The final summary is exceptionally well-structured: reproduction steps, fix options, test case, and information gaps all make it immediately developer-usable
Confirming system-wide scope in Q3 efficiently ruled out account-specific delivery issues before closing
notable_weaknesses: The accuracy hypothesis conflates internal queue congestion with external email-provider rate limiting — a developer chasing the SMTP rate-limit angle could waste time before finding the internal priority queue issue
The agent never asked when the digest feature launched, relying solely on the reporter's uncertain 'around the same time' recollection rather than independently pinning the 1-week timeline
most_insightful_question: Can you tell me more about when that digest email typically arrives in your inbox? For example, does it show up around 9am, and do you notice the delayed task notifications trickling in shortly after it, or is there a big gap between the two?
missed_opportunities: Ask when exactly the digest feature was launched (to independently confirm the 1-week onset rather than relying on reporter memory)
Ask approximately how many users receive the digest email (to quantify queue pressure and strengthen the congestion hypothesis)
Ask whether anyone has tried disabling the digest or checking the TaskFlow dashboard as a workaround to confirm assignments are being created on time server-side
